title: [doc] add more explanation for `--userns keep-id` about uid mapping
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

It is more about a question of understanding uid mapping in the `podman run --userns keep-id` command.

* https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-run.1.html
* https://www.redhat.com/sysadmin/user-flag-rootless-containers 

```` .bash
~ $ id
uid=5658(guest) gid=54000(users) groups=54000(users)
~ $ cat /etc/subuid
podman:1:999
podman:1001:64535
guest:100000:65536
~ $ podman run -it --userns keep-id alpine
~ $ cat /proc/1/uid_map  # inside container
         0          1       5658
      5658          0          1
      5659       5659      59878
# compare 
$ podman run -it alpine
/ # cat /proc/1/uid_map  # inside container
         0       5658          1
         1     100000      65536
````

It is easily to understand uid mapping output for `root`, but for `keep-id`, how to understand the output.


**Describe the results you received:**


**Describe the results you expected:**

I expect it can be well described in related documentation.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
$ podman version
Client:       Podman Engine
Version:      4.1.1
API Version:  4.1.1
Go Version:   go1.18.3
Built:        Wed Jun 22 16:17:44 2022
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

**Output of `podman info`:**

```
$ podman version
Client:       Podman Engine
Version:      4.0.2
API Version:  4.0.2
Go Version:   go1.17.7

Built:      Tue Apr 19 12:16:32 2022
OS/Arch:    linux/amd64
```

comments.body:
The issue is you are seeing the mapping within a mapping.

```
$ podman run -it alpine
/ # cat /proc/1/uid_map  # inside container
         0       5658          1
         1     100000      65536
```
Is only doing a single mapping, basically this is joining the default user namespace.

When you use 
```
~ $ podman run -it --userns keep-id alpine
~ $ cat /proc/1/uid_map  # inside container
         0          1       5658
      5658          0          1
      5659       5659      59878
```
Podman is doing a remapping of the user namespace which has 65k UIDs.

It mapps UID 1->5658 (100000-105657) to 0->5657
It mappes your UID 0 (5658) back to UID 5658
Then it mapps the rest of the usernamespace to itself.
5659-59878 (105658-106536) -> 5659-59878

The UIDs inside of the () are the real UIDS on the host outside of all user namespaces.

I am not good at this part, so I am trying to find the command output to understand uid `5658` (inside) is mapped to uid (`105657 ` ?? ) outside

````
$ podman run -d ubuntu sleep 80000
$ podman top -l user,huser
USER        HUSER
root          5658               <== this is clear to me
$ podman run -d --userns keep-id ubuntu sleep 80000 
$ podman top -l user,huser
USER        HUSER
guest        guest             <== ?
````

do we have parameter to show uid instead of name, here is always `guest`, or some improvements for `HUSER` to have extra `HUID`?

Now I understand more about it, I explained for myself below

My host env 

```` .bash
~ $ id
uid=5658(guest) gid=54000(users) groups=54000(users)
~ $ cat /etc/subuid
podman:1:999
podman:1001:64535
guest:100000:65536
````

## For simple case, use default `--userns ""`

```` .bash
$ podman run -it ubuntu
$ podman exec -t -l cat /proc/1/uid_map
         0       5658          1
         1     100000      65536
$ podman run -d ubuntu sleep 80000
$ podman top -l user,huser
USER        HUSER
root        5658  
$ podman run -u 1000 -d ubuntu sleep 80000
$ podman top -l user,huser
USER        HUSER
1000        100999   
````

Now we can see the mapping

| inside container  | host | uid_map | notes |
| ------------- | ------------- | ----- | ---- |
|  root (0)  | guest(5658) | row 1 | 0 -> 5658 |
| 1000  | 100999| row 2 |  1->subuid base 100000, so 1000 -> subuid base 100000 + 1000 -1 

## For special case, use `--userns keep-id`

```` .bash
$ podman run -d --userns keep-id ubuntu sleep 80000
$ podman exec -t -l cat /proc/1/uid_map
         0          1       5658
      5658          0          1
      5659       5659      59878
$ podman top -l user,huser
USER        HUSER
guest        guest  
$ podman run -u 1000 -d --userns keep-id  ubuntu sleep 80000
$ podman top -l user,huser
USER        HUSER
1000        101000   
$ podman run -u 10000 -d --userns keep-id  ubuntu sleep 80000
$ podman top -l user,huser
USER        HUSER
10000       109999   
````

mapping for `5658` is special, others are calculate according to the able (see @rhatdan 's comments)

| inside container  | host | uid_map | notes |
| ------------- | ------------- | -----| -- |
|  guest(5658)  | guest(5658) | row 2 | it is special case with remapping  |
| 1000  | 101000| row 1 | 1000 < 5658,  0 .. 10000, 1000 .. 101000 ?? |
| 10000  | 109999 | row 3 | 10000 > 5659  ??  |

Another simple way to check the mapping is writing to the file 

```` .bash
$ cd $HOME 
$ chmod 777 .  # u can change to other folder
$ podman run -u 5659 -v $HOME:$HOME -d --userns keep-id ubuntu touch $HOME/abc
$ ls -al abc
-rw-r--r-- 1 105658 100000 0 Aug 11 07:44 abc
$ rm -f abc &&  podman run -u 0 -v $HOME:$HOME -d --userns keep-id ubuntu touch $HOME/abc && ls -al abc
-rw-r--r-- 1 100000 100000 0 Aug 11 07:51 abc
$ rm -f abc &&  podman run -u 1000 -v $HOME:$HOME -d --userns keep-id ubuntu touch $HOME/abc && ls -al abc
-rw-r--r-- 1 101000 100000 0 Aug 11 08:02 abc
$ rm -f abc &&  podman run -u 10000 -v $HOME:$HOME -d --userns keep-id ubuntu touch $HOME/abc && ls -al abc
-rw-r--r-- 1 109999 100000 0 Aug 11 08:02 abc
````

What I expected in `uid_map` is simple mapping

````
$ podman exec -t -l cat /proc/1/uid_map # below is my expectation, faked output
         0        100000       5657
      5658          5658          1
      5659        105658      59878
````

it is not so straight to understanding the mapping in `uid_map`, but it is good enough for me (need time to learn)
